q: A capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of a magnetic field.
A capacitor stores electrical energy in the form of an electric field.
When a capacitor is charged, charge is transferred from a conductor at higher potential to a conductor at lower potential.
Work has been done for the transfer of charge from a lower potential conductor to a higher potential conductor.
During the charging process, the magnitude of the charge on the two conductors increases gradually.
The potential difference between the conductors decreases gradually as the capacitor charges.
The energy stored by the capacitor can be analyzed from the $Q-V$ graph.
The work done in charging the capacitor is given by the shaded area under the curve of a $Q-V$ graph.
The area under the $Q-V$ curve that represents work done is a shaded rectangle.
The work done in charging the capacitor is equal to $\frac{1}{2}QV$.
Before a capacitor is charged, each of its conductors already carries a full maximum charge.
Before a capacitor is charged, the potential difference between the two conductors is zero.
The average potential difference of the capacitor before and after it is charged is $\frac{V}{2}$.
The work done $W$ for transferring a charge of $Q$ between two conductors is given by $W = \bar{V}Q$.
The electrical energy stored by the capacitor is kept in the form of a gravitational field.
Since $C = Q/V$, the energy $W$ of a capacitor can be expressed as $W = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$.
The energy $W$ of a capacitor can be expressed as $W = \frac{1}{2}\frac{Q^2}{C}$.
Placing a resistor in the charging circuit speeds up the charging process.
Placing a resistor in the charging circuit slows down the charging process.
The greater the values of resistance and capacitance, the longer it takes to charge the capacitor.
The greater the values of resistance and capacitance, the shorter it takes to charge the capacitor.
During charging, the capacitor voltage rises with time according to the equation $V_c = V_0(1 - e^{-t/RC})$.
The product of the resistance $R$ and the capacitance $C$ is called the time constant $\tau$.
The time constant $\tau$ of a capacitor charging or discharging process is measured in seconds.
The time constant $\tau$ of a capacitor charging or discharging process is measured in Farads.
After being charged, a capacitor can be discharged through a load resistor $R_L$.
During discharging, the voltage of the capacitor falls off with time according to the equation $V_c = V_0(e^{-t/R_L C})$.
